summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software developers should adopt the portfolio practice common among graphic designers, bringing samples of their work to job interviews rather than relying solely on resume keywords. He observes that developers rarely show their actual work during interviews, despite the obvious benefits of 'show, don't tell.' The post emphasizes that what matters most isn't just having a portfolio, but demonstrating genuine excitement about past projects. Atwood concludes that candidates who can both show their work and articulate what made it fun and challenging stand out dramatically from the competition.
key_points: Developers should borrow the portfolio concept from graphic designers and bring work samples to interviews
Resumes full of buzzwords like 'enterprise' and 'architect' are far less compelling than actual demonstrated work
A portfolio doesn't require web or UI work—well-designed console applications or clever code snippets count
Showing genuine enthusiasm for past projects is more important than the portfolio itself
Being able to communicate what made projects fun and challenging is the ultimate differentiator
money_quotes: Anyone can put together boilerplate resume text, full of assertive verbs and fancy keywords. Blah blah enterprise blah blah strategic blah blah architect blah blah.
No, you don't have to be working on a web application or website to have something worthy of putting in a portfolio.
As a fellow developer, I can appreciate the beauty of a well-designed console application, or a clever applet with hardly any interface at all.
If you've written code you're particularly proud of, show me that.
The portfolio is important, but what's more important is that you are excited about what you worked on.
If you took the time to highlight the cool stuff and bring it with you, that already puts you far ahead of every other candidate I've ever interviewed.
key_insight: Showing your actual work and genuine enthusiasm for it will differentiate you far more than any polished resume full of industry buzzwords.
